speaker: interviewer
text: The date is November 14, '96, survivor Charles Salt. The interviewer's Ian Kerzen. It's in London, England and the language is English. My name is Ian Kerzen. It's November the 14th, 1996. I'm with Charles Salt in London, England. Can I ask your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Charles Salt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10th of March, 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- and which city do you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: London.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: London.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, Charles I'd like to start by asking some questions about your early life before the war. Can you tell me what was life like at home in the pre-war period?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a working life, that-- that people went to work. Even if you's good at school and you had a chance to go to, say, to a grammar schools or university, parents didn't bother to send you because they couldn't afford to send you to a university, as today you can get a grant from university. In those days, you didn't have a grant, and you left school when you was 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as soon as you left school at 14, you wanted to go to work, earn a few shillings-- in those days, it was shillings-- to supplement the budget, so to speak. What was your school like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an LCC elementary school, London [INAUDIBLE], which has being abolished since. It was elementary school. It was, I would say, 90% Jewish. The teachers were Jewish, and we observed all Jewish holidays and Fridays we used to finish at 2:30.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your parents do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked in [? tailoring ?]. My father used to work in [? tailoring, ?] but mother never worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she was in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have his own business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he used to work-- go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she's-- she doesn't work now. She's 95.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, in-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she was hair dressing.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was hair dressing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ladies hair dresser.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some elements of family life? Did you-- were you are religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes. We were a religious family. In Eastern London, more or less everyone was religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: To what sort of degree? Were you devout? Did you go to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they used to go Friday nights and Saturdays and all Jewish holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this very community spirited?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very community spirited. If you wanted to go somewhere, or you wanted to go there, you didn't make an appointment like today. You phoned them up. Oh, you in? Can I come round? You just went in. Or if you happened to be playing, say, with the children next door, if you just-- just stayed there for tea or supper, whatever it was. You didn't go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how were the relationships with the neighbors? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, good, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very friendly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very good. Like one big family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it a very Jewish area where you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very Jewish area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was your relationships, as-- as a community, with the people outside? Was there a lot of cross contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was no animosity?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the general feeling of anti-Semitism in London? Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was in the '30s, after Hitler come to power, sort of took on. And-- and from that, you see, then Mosley started his fascist party and by strange coincidence, he was the former labor cabinet minister. And then when he [? got ?] up, he formed his own party, British Union Fascists, and he contested his seat in 1931 for [INAUDIBLE] and by some strange twist, he put up Kid Lewis, who was a Jewish boxer. He put him up in the East End to try to get votes for him, but it flopped. I think he only got 136 votes or something. He didn't get many votes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And why people joined his party, they became fascists because there was a lot of unemployment at the time-- excuse me-- and if you joined the party, you got paid. And your pay was more than if you joined the army. So people who went out to work would sooner join his party, get a uniform, and be paid more than if you joined the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the-- on the domestic front, did you have a lot of-- were the festivals together? Were you a large group of--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, each family was separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on, say, occasions like Pesach, Passover, were there a lot of-- did you have a big gathering? And was this a--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a small--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everybody had their own.

speaker: interviewer
text: And moving onto the earlier, your early career, what was your-- when you left school, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was apprentice to a gent's tailor because he was very Orthodox. Friday we used to finish half past 3 o'clock and didn't work on the Sabbaths or Jewish holidays. And the feeling was at the time with, like, the parents, when a child left school, you should always teach him a trade, what they called a trade.

speaker: interviewee
text: He should either be a be a tailor or [INAUDIBLE] or a barber or something. Because the persecution in Germany, people were leaving the country. So wherever you went, if you got persecuted, if you went to America or Australia, you had something in your hands. You could earn your own bread and butter, so to speak. You didn't have to [INAUDIBLE]. You can say, oh, I'm a tailor. I'm a hairdresser or something. You could do something. So the idea was to teach you a trade, you see? So I was apprentice to a gent's tailor. Were you aware of any-- you mentioned earlier. Were you aware of any persecution in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in the East End, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the-- of outside Europe or did you know anything about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the '30's when Hitler come to power, '33.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of information did you have? What-- what did you know about what was going on in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was only what we read in the papers at the time, that he-- he came to power and they started all this persecution.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when you were at work, again, was that a very Jewish environment or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very Jewish. Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: All the customers were Jewish, yeah, mainly Jewish. 99.9% of the customers were-- were Jewish. Of course, in those days, you had private tailors. If you wanted a suit, you went to a private tailor, had to make the measure, as opposed to today, you go into a shop and you buy it ready made. Those days are gone, unless you got to [INAUDIBLE], you want it made to measure. And the first-- the first day, the first week I was apprenticed, like when I left school and went to work, the governor said, if you behave yourself next week like you did this week, I'll give you sixpence. So the first week I got nothing and for the next six months I got sixpence.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's sixpence a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sixpence a week. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that a reasonable--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't. But you see, you either pay the premium to learn a trade or you didn't pay the premium and you didn't get paid. And that was the-- and then after six months, I got a shilling a week. But occasionally, if you delivered a suit, you might get a tip, sixpence or something. But other than that-- but it taught you a good trade, you see? You can always go and earn your money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other aspirations, any other things you wanted to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because you-- even if you did, you knew you wasn't going to get anywhere unless you had a sponsor. You see, if you had someone to pay for you to go to university or something, you wasn't going to get anywhere so you didn't bother. And some people didn't like it. You see, some people don't don't like-- like, being sponsored by other people, say, I don't want anyone to give me anything. I want to get there but the sweat of my own brow, so to speak. So know one can ever point the finger at you and say, well, if it wasn't for me, you wouldn't be where you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: And outside work, what sort of activities did you get involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you'd-- you'd go sometimes to a meeting, a political meeting or something, that-- of course, we-- not because you was politically minded, it was some kind of entertainment, with the heckling. They're more just like professional hecklers. It was like an [LAUGHS] entertainment. So people would go to meetings or-- or they'd go dancing. Or there'd be--

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of meetings were they? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, conservative meetings or labor-party meetings. And people go heckling all the time. And it would be like a-- fun. Of course, they didn't-- never let the speaker speak because they were always asking difficult questions and arguments and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find that these were of interest to you? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the politics side?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was just to pass the time away, that's all. It was like a free evening or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people used to conduct meetings on the street corners. And then sometimes, if you go down [? to my ?] [? chapel ?] two people start talking and then people gather around, then they start talking. And then the two people that started originally, they'd walk away and everybody was arguing [LAUGHS] amongst themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was, during this period, I believe there was a lot of anti-Semitism in the areas around the East End.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, afterwards, like in '35, '36, then it became a bit more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did this affect you in any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the East End, there was a fairly strong Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the English fascists didn't come into your area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was only the time when the-- that the-- Moseley wanted to go through Cable Street in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were still in the East End, and everyone turned out to stop him, which they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the family side, were your parents very-- very much in keeping with this sort of same sorts of things? Did they have--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: A good-- Did they go out much? Or did they have--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they used to go visiting people, you know, that you knew from the old country, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody lived together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your-- were your parents originally from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did they come over to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1900s somewhere. They got married in 1908 in [INAUDIBLE] Square Synagogue. 1908 they were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when- -at what point were you-- did you become involved with the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when war broke out, I got called up in 19-- February 5, 1940. I went for a medical in December, and then they sent-- I called them up, [INAUDIBLE] the post law, what they called the king's shilling, the time they gave-- they paid you to-- you got called up on Thursday and pay day was Friday so you got paid for one day. So you get what was called the king's shilling. They sent you a post law that told me to report. I was fortunate. I was told to report in Hounslow Barracks, where they were all fusiliers because that was, in the First World War, there was a Jewish battalion, they were all fusiliers so they knew all the holidays and everything, the Jewish holidays. And I was able to go home nearly every day from Whitechapel Station to Hounslow on the Underground. So I was able to go back. So I was quite fortunate in that respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say they were-- you were segregated in a Jewish area or Jewish brigade?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was a Jewish battalion in the First World War, and they were all fusiliers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you go to the Royal Fusiliers Museum in the Tower of London, they've got a life-sized cardboard cut out of David Ben-Gurion, who was a corporal in 1915, and he didn't like English politics so he left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like for you in the first few days in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very strange. If you're coming out of the kosher home, and a-- a kosher house, and then you're suddenly thrown into a thing like that, you go in the morning for breakfast, they're having sausages or kidneys, or a mug of tea and bread and butter, and the smell was horrific. You couldn't eat it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was fortunate. When you get there, after a few days, you go to see a welfare officer, and he calls all the new recruits together, like, individually, says, have you any problems? There wasn't many that were married, a very few were married, or have you got a mortgage to pay or any problems? I said-- I said, no. He said, nothing else? I said, well, there's just one thing. I'm of Jewish faith, and I don't eat the meat. So he said, well, if there'd be a quorum of 10, that they order special meals but for one, they can't order the whole military organization for it. But he said, you go to the cookhouse lunch time, whenever there's meals, and you tell them I sent you, and they should give you a vegetarian meal. So I used to do that. [? You see. ?] And they would offer you these facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about for the holidays? We're you allowed to actually--

speaker: interviewee
text: You was allowed to go. A notice always come through from the War Office, all Jewish personnel who wish to observe the festival of Passover or the Jewish New Year, they'd get a circular from the war office from the senior Jewish chaplain, and they knew fully well been a formal Jewish regiment there, that you applied for it subject-- the army has got funny ways of talking, that they throw in French words. Instead of like, subject's conditions that prevail, it will say, subject to the exigencies of the services permitting, he should be allowed to go home in lieu of and not in addition to normal leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you were in some way penalized, but at least you had the opportunity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so, I used to go home. I was fortunate I was able to go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as your-- as your career in the army progressed, what was the next sort of stage? You were in basic training, where did you go from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from Hounslow, that was like [? week's ?] training, you went over to Hounslow Heath. This was before Heathrow was ever thought of. Heathrow didn't exist at the time. It was Hounslow Heath. And there you was at what they called advanced training, and from there you went to battalion, and then from there you went to whatever you--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was your advanced training? What sort of things did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: You went on a rifle range in [? Perp, ?] right? They used to show you how to use a rifle or machine guns or whatever. And they use to have target, like, you had to pass out [? that ?] you got shot. Well, what happened, the old soldiers knew the trick, you see? Some-- [LAUGHS] some soldiers wasn't very good at aiming. They wouldn't hit the target, so what they done. And if you didn't get a certain score, you didn't get an extra sixpence, what they called proficiency pay. So what they did, if nobody hit the target, they put a pencil through the target so when they checked, they said, yes, he's-- he's hit past it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you get your extra sixpence.

speaker: interviewee
text: You get your extra sixpence a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- how long before you were actually--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, that was eight weeks in Hounslow Heath, then you joined the battalion. Well, that went down from February down through March. I went home for Passover March, April. And then the other May we got posted to a battalion. Well, in between, the Dunkirk disaster cropped up. And that's where we was supposed to be going to France, it got stopped because they were bringing the troops back. So we got held-- I think it was in Stanmore we went. And we was waiting, nobody knew where-- where to go, whether to send them to France or not to send them because they were coming back. And they didn't admit it. All they said was, everything is going according to plan. And they were coming back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we joined the battalion in a little town near Christchurch called Cattistock. Never heard of it? I don't think it's on the map, but they found these places in the Countess of Ilchester's state-- estate. We were put in the horse stables. Of course, they had horses in those days. We were put n the stables and we joined the battalion, what remained of the battalion. We made it up and said that we had nothing, just a-- a few rifles and some ammunition. And the troops that came back, they had no clothes or nothing. Lost everything. So were you closely associated with some of the returning troops?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And helped them out and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had-- they said, well, you've been kitted out in the depot, so you've got to help out the ones that come back and share everything you've got with them until such times as supplies come through.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there how did your--

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we stayed in Cattistock, and then when everybody come back, when the Dunkirk affair was over, we were posted to the Isle of Wight because they said-- this was in June-- and they said, if the Germans are going to launch an invasion of England, they're going to go for the Isle of Wight first because that is the nearest place they can make a landing before going to the mainland. They won't go for the mainland, they'll go for the Isle of Wight first and build up and launch the invasion on the mainland in March. See, everything has to go to a timetable. They took, the Germans took the Channel Islands but then over here they thought, well, it's-- it's no value, the Channel Islands, anyway. It's not going to win the war either way, so they just let them get away with it. But the Isle of Wight was a key point, and we used to go there-- in the night, during the night, watch and see if there's an invasion coming or something, and in the morning we'd go back to the billets. And-- but then they come round and they said, everybody should have a day off. What they call a day off, instead of going out every night until the morning, you helped the cook. So you didn't go out, you had the night's sleep, and you helped the cook in the morning make breakfast and making dinner during the day time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he come round to me, says, would you like to have off Friday night to Saturday morning or Saturday morning to Sunday morning? Of course, we had the Sabbath, so they said you can have the option which one. It's not much difference. I'm not going anywhere. So I said, all right, I'll go for Saturday morning until Sunday. At least I have all day Saturday off. And we were sleeping on the beaches at the time because the-- the invasion scare was at its height. And also the intelligence people, they used to do these reconnaissance visits, the RAF, and they noticed in the [INAUDIBLE], the Calais area, there was hundreds of barges. And nobody could work it out. Why do they need the barges? Why did the Germans need the barges? They're not saving them up like dinky toys. And they couldn't work it out. And then one Sunday morning, I was helping the cook. An officer come in, he said, what are you doing? I said, I'm helping the cook making breakfast. He said, if you don't go out there, the Germans will have you for breakfast. He said, there's an inviting going on out there. So I said, well, how they getting on? [LAUGHS] He said, get out there quick. So I went out, so a loud cheer went up. England's last hope's arrived. [LAUGHS] So anyway, this was 7 o'clock in the morning. At 9:00-- at 9 o'clock in the morning, the all clear went so we left the dugouts on the coast, and we-- we went back. And then a few hours later, an order comes through, the beaches are out of bounds to all ranks. Before, they used to tell you to while away your time, go swimming in the sea or whatever, but it was out of bounds for all ranks. So in a certain way, the word's coming up, well, what's going on? So it turns out that the Germans, they had all these barges. They filled them up with troops, and they did launch an invasion against the Isle of Wight, but how do you get the barges across the channel? Barges can't go by themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: They got the Italian navy, [INAUDIBLE] torpedo boats, to tow them across the channel but the rope towing them was behind the barge. And the idea was when they come about a mile offshore, they part, the two barges part, catapulting the barge into the Isle of Wight. And the speed of that would've been between 50 to 60 miles an hour. And if they come in like that, there was nothing-- you could stop them. And we had nothing to stop them. All we had was a rifle and 15 rounds of ammunition, nothing else. There was no anti-tank guns, anything, nothing. Just 15 rounds of ammunition, that's all we had. And we had a stretch of beach, nine miles, I think it was from Ventnor to St. Catherine's Point, the whole length of the beach there. And the idea was, when they part they catapult these barges in. Well, as soon as the alarm went up-- so Churchill called an emergency meeting with the chiefs of staff and the navy and the air force, and they threw everything they had into it, the battle ships, the cruisers, whatever they had, the [INAUDIBLE]-- everything, because this was the big showdown they were waiting for. They threw everything they had into it. Well, when the Italians saw these big battle ships coming up and the cruisers, they unhooked the barges, turned round and went for Marseilles. So the barges were left floating in the channel because they can't move themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, an argument cropped up. The RAF said we want to finish it off, the navy said we want to finish it off. So at the end the day, Churchill said let the RAF finish off. Of course, that's just the boost they need for the morale after the Battle of Britain. He said, that's the best thing that they can have. So the RAF made a circle of incendiary bombs around the barges and gradually they closed in. And they were all bones and the dead bodies are washed up in the Isle of Wight. That's why we was stopped from going to the beaches. And moving forward, what were the next sort of major series of events that happened in your army career?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next series-- after that, the-- that was on a Sunday. The following day on the Monday, Churchill sent a memo to the then chief of staff, Lord-- Lord Alan Brooke, he was chief. He sent him a memo, prepare for the invasion to Europe. And that took four years of careful planning. The Normandy landings took four years of very, very careful planning, rehearsals, and nothing should go wrong. And that's when it started, the day after, because they knew if that invasion failed, they can't launch another one. Had they come in June 1940, they could have just walked into England, but then it-- they couldn't do anything else. And then after that, I-- we went-- after six months, we went back to the mainland and Newbury Racecourse.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just training, what they used to say, training, more to give us a rest because we was there six weeks, six months. It was six months on the coast.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find these-- was it very monotonous, very boring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very boring, very monotonous. Just-- that's why I've never gone back to the Isle of Wight. We just stood down the-- on the beaches and waited for an invasion to happen, which is not a pleasant thing and nothing's happening. And then the night, it gets very cold. In the day time, it's very hot and the night time gets very cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any entertainment laid on [INTERPOSING VOICES] or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Afterwards they said we can go into Ventnor once a week for a few hours, but the army, they have their own shows, you know, the army shows. They used to make their own entertainment. [INAUDIBLE] wasn't started up yet in 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was no-- nothing really for you to do at all--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. It was very boring. You was just-- you didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did this go on for?

speaker: interviewee
text: This went on till December. December we went to the, shifted to Newbury, onto the race course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was bitterly cold. Christmas, 1940, it was very cold. All the water froze up and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go onto-- to the next stage. Where were you after the race course in Newbury?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I got posted back to-- no, I was in-- I had some problem with my leg and there was a regulation, if you were away from your unit 21 days, you got sent back to your depot. Well, I knew about this regulation. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So after 21 days, I was in a place called Tidworth, Tidworth Hospital, military hospital, I got posted back to the depot in Hounslow so I went back to Hounslow. And from there, I went to-- from there, you get posted to a draft, whatever draft was going where they needed new troops. You said, it was luck of the draw. And I got posted back to the West Kents for some reason, because there was a draft on that to the Middle East.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there-- you have to pass a medical and the doctor there said, oh, you've been in the hospital 22 days. You're not fit for this type of thing, and I got sent back to Lingfield, another race course, Lingfield.

speaker: interviewee
text: At Lingfield, when we got there, there was an MCC flag flying and the old-fashioned [? Lucas ?] head lights, all the way round the camp. So, I said to the drive, I said, what are these lights for? He said, oh, when there's an air raid, we flood light the camp so the Germans know that the prison's over here. It's the safest place in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the flag was an MCC flag was because the colonel in charge of the-- Lieutenant Colonel French, his father was a field marshal in the First World War so he had a bit of sway. He said, if you play cricket, you'll be all right here. And he-- he was a member of the MCC so he had this flag flying. But after that, I got posted back to Chatham. I got posted back to Chatham, and we went to a unit there where it was all odds and ends, people who had been in prison, people gone absent, difficult soldiers, and people who had been in the hospital for a long time or not fit for normal service. All these troops want to Chatham, what they call a home detail unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. I think we'll call a halt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, yeah.
